text:
MRI THORACIC AND LUMBAR SPINE WITH AND WITHOUT CONTRAST
(Whole Spine Protocol)

CLINICAL INDICATION:
71-year-old male with metastatic castration-resistant prostate adenocarcinoma (Gleason 4+5=9, BRCA2 germline mutation, AR amplification on somatic NGS). Progressive disease on enza with new bone lesions identified on recent whole-body bone scan at T10, right posterior 6th rib, left iliac wing, and right posterior iliac crest. New onset mid-back pain grade 1-2. Exam obtained to evaluate for spinal cord compromise, epidural disease, and characterize extent of vertebral involvement prior to treatment planning.

TECHNIQUE:
Multiplanar, multisequence MRI of the cervical, thoracic, and lumbar spine performed on a 3.0 Tesla magnet. Sequences include sagittal T1-weighted, sagittal STIR, sagittal T2-weighted, axial T2-weighted through levels of concern, and sagittal/axial post-contrast T1-weighted fat-saturated sequences following administration of 10 mL IV gadolinium contrast (gadobutrol). No adverse reaction to contrast. Comparison made to available prior cross-sectional imaging including CT chest/abdomen/pelvis and bone scan from the same time period.

FINDINGS:

Cervical Spine:
Alignment intact. No acute fracture or subluxation. No abnormal marrow signal to suggest metastatic involvement throughout the cervical vertebral bodies or posterior elements. Cervical spinal cord: normal caliber and signal intensity without intrinsic cord lesion. Mild multilevel degenerative disc disease at C5-6 and C6-7 with small posterior disc osteophyte complexes, without significant canal or foraminal stenosis. No epidural collection.

Thoracic Spine:
Alignment is maintained throughout the thoracic spine. No acute kyphotic deformity or collapse.

T10 vertebral body: There is extensive T1 hypointense, STIR hyperintense signal replacing the normal marrow fat signal throughout the T10 vertebral body and extending into the right T10 pedicle. Post-contrast imaging demonstrates avid, heterogeneous enhancement. The overall morphology of the vertebral body is preserved without cortical disruption or height loss -- no pathological compression fracture. There is a small amount of paravertebral soft tissue conspicuity best seen on axial T2 and post-contrast sequences, representing a thin rim of peritumoral/paravertebral soft tissue change measuring approximately 3-4 mm in maximal thickness on the right posterolateral aspect; this does not extend into the spinal canal. The thecal sac and spinal cord are not displaced. The spinal cord signal is normal at this level without evidence of cord edema or myelopathy. The epidural fat plane is intact circumferentially. Neural foramina at T10 are patent bilaterally.

T11 and T12: No definitive abnormal marrow signal. Mild scattered T1 heterogeneity at T12 likely related to age-related fatty infiltration versus very early infiltrative change, equivocal, and below the threshold for confident metastatic designation. STIR signal at T12 not convincingly elevated.

Remaining thoracic vertebrae (T1-T9, T11): No convincing abnormal marrow signal to suggest metastatic involvement. Normal fatty marrow throughout. No posterior element involvement beyond what is described at T10.

Lumbar Spine:
Alignment maintained. No spondylolisthesis.

L3 vertebral body: There is subtle T1 hypointense signal in the right posterior aspect of the L3 vertebral body with corresponding mild STIR hyperintensity. Post-contrast enhancement is present but mild. No cortical disruption. No associated soft tissue mass. No epidural extension. This pattern is most consistent with early marrow infiltration, most likely representing early metastatic involvement in the appropriate clinical context. The overall size of involvement appears small, occupying less than 25% of the vertebral body volume by estimation. Spinal canal and bilateral neural foramina at L3 are widely patent.

L1, L2, L4, L5, S1: No convincing abnormal marrow signal. Mild age-related degenerative endplate changes at L4-5 and L5-S1. No fracture.

Conus medullaris: Normal position at the inferior aspect of T12/superior L1 level. Normal signal. No syrinx.

Cauda equina: Nerve roots appear normal in course and caliber. No intradural lesion.

Soft Tissues:
No significant paraspinal or prevertebral soft tissue mass beyond the thin paravertebral change described at T10. No psoas muscle signal abnormality. Partially visualized kidneys appear unremarkable. Partially visualized posterior elements of the pelvis at the inferior aspect of the lumbar series again show abnormal marrow signal in the right posterior iliac crest consistent with the known metastatic lesion identified on prior bone scan and PSMA PET/CT, not further characterized on this spine-targeted study.

IMPRESSION:

1. T10 vertebral body metastasis with extensive marrow replacement and mild paravertebral soft tissue change on the right posterolateral aspect. No epidural tumor extension. Spinal cord is not compressed and demonstrates normal signal. No pathological fracture. Given the proximity of this lesion to the spinal cord and the degree of involvement, radiation oncology consultation is appropriate for treatment planning.

2. L3 vertebral body with early focal marrow infiltration in the right posterior aspect, most consistent with early metastatic involvement given the overall clinical context. No cord compromise, no fracture, no epidural disease at this level.

3. Equivocal marrow heterogeneity at T12; this is below the threshold for definitive metastatic designation and may represent early infiltrative change. Continued surveillance recommended.

4. No spinal cord compression at any level. No myelopathic signal change. Epidural fat planes intact throughout.

5. No pathological vertebral fracture identified.

6. Multilevel cervical and lumbar degenerative disc disease without significant stenosis, likely contributing to background axial discomfort.
